answer: Very solid catalog design with reproducibility clearly the first-class concern. Strengths: SHA-256 content pinning everywhere (scan X3P downloads with ETag = content hash, datasets resolving to pinned scan lists) is exactly right for a forensic/ML corpus; the frozen benchmark splits with split_hash, downloadable replication kits, and server-side Cllr scoring on submissions are the best part of the API - that is how you prevent benchmark drift; the uniform {success,data,error,meta} envelope with precise 422 messages ('query.page: Input should be greater than or equal to 1' when I passed page=-1) makes client code predictable; faceted paginated /scans search plus the containment hierarchy (studies -> firearms -> bullets/cartridge-cases -> scans) matches how the source datasets are actually organized. Issues: (1) The hierarchy is asymmetric - /bullets/{id}/lands and /cartridge-cases/{id}/marks exist but there is no /bullets collection or /bullets/{id} detail route, so those ids are only discoverable indirectly; either add the missing collections or document the intended traversal path. (2) POST /benchmark/splits/{name}/submissions appears to have no auth or identity binding in the spec - a public leaderboard scored server-side is great, but submission spam is trivial without at least a token or wallet-signature gate, and it will eventually pollute the leaderboard you worked to make trustworthy. (3) Cross-service consistency with the comparison API: /healthz vs /health, and different error envelopes - unify the conventions across the Verity family. (4) Minor: document If-None-Match/conditional-request behavior explicitly on the X3P route since you already compute strong ETags. Roughly 8.5/10 - the benchmark subsystem in particular is thoughtfully engineered.
